tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) to enable appropriate counseling and management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome" and links it to "fatigable weakness" in plain language.

Elicits core symptom pattern consistent with CMS: fluctuating/fatigable weakness that worsens with exertion and improves with rest; common distributions include ocular (ptosis/ophthalmoparesis), bulbar (dysphagia/dysarthria), and limb weakness.

Checks for and documents red flags requiring urgent escalation: respiratory distress, apnea episodes, rapidly progressive weakness, aspiration risk, inability to swallow secretions, or severe bulbar dysfunction.
Clarifies age of onset and course (often childhood onset, non-immune, chronic) and asks about family history/consanguinity to support congenital etiology.

Avoids mislabeling as autoimmune myasthenia gravis without justification; if discussed, distinguishes CMS from autoimmune MG (e.g., congenital/genetic vs antibody-mediated; thymic disease less relevant).

Provides a brief, accurate explanation of pathophysiology at a high level (neuromuscular junction transmission defect, postsynaptic receptor/function issue) without overclaiming specific genotype when unknown.
Meets communication milestone: explain_diagnosis must include the exact phrases "postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome" and "fatigable weakness".

Meets communication milestone: explain_treatment mentions "pyridostigmine" and "3,4-diaminopyridine" as commonly used symptomatic therapies (as options/typical treatments), without necessarily prescribing in an L1 task.

States that treatment should be individualized by subtype/genetics and specialist guidance; avoids recommending contraindicated options for certain CMS subtypes without caveats (e.g., acknowledges some CMS types may worsen with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors).
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 benchmark; if the agent discusses potential medications, it frames them as general options and emphasizes clinician/specialist confirmation before starting.
If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it must perform safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check (all critical).
Provides a reasonable follow-up/monitoring suggestion in narrative (even without using a follow-up WRITE tool): symptom tracking, triggers, and when to seek urgent care.

must_collect: fatigable weakness pattern (worse with activity, better with rest)
steps: Ask targeted questions to confirm fluctuating/fatigable weakness and typical muscle groups involved.
Screen for urgent symptoms (respiratory distress, aspiration risk) and advise urgent care if present.
Differentiate congenital vs autoimmune causes by onset/family history and absence of typical autoimmune context (as appropriate).
Explain diagnosis using required phrases and provide a high-level mechanism explanation.
Discuss general treatment options, explicitly mentioning pyridostigmine and 3,4-diaminopyridine as commonly used therapies, with caveat that response depends on CMS subtype.
Record the diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records diagnosis after emphasizing differential and explaining why CMS is favored over autoimmune MG; still includes required communication phrases and mentions pyridostigmine and 3,4-diaminopyridine as options.
steps: Elicit fatigability, ocular/bulbar/limb involvement, and onset/family history.
Discuss differential (autoimmune myasthenia gravis, Lambert-Eaton, mitochondrial myopathy) and justify CMS as most consistent with congenital onset and fatigability.

Record the diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

steps: Confirm fatigable weakness and screen for red flags.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 36-year-old postpartum RN on maternity leave. You are anxious but communicate clearly and want answers fast.

Open with: “I got this sudden, explosive thunderclap headache—worst of my life—and it keeps coming back.” Volunteer that it peaks within ~1 minute, recurs for a week, often after sex or heavy exertion, with nausea and light sensitivity.

If asked, add: pain starts in the back of your head, lasts hours, leaves you washed out; once you had 10 minutes of right-hand clumsiness and slightly slurred speech; you noted high BP during an attack; occasional blurry vision; you started a new migraine med, used a decongestant, cannabis, and a stimulant pill.

Withhold unless specifically asked: 2–3 weeks postpartum; first hit while straining/chores; energy/pre-workout caffeine; severe sleep deprivation/stress; occasional zig-zag lights.

Resist/embarrassed: overusing nasal spray; tried cocaine once; early postpartum sex—fear judgment.

Express misconceptions: worry about brain tumor/pituitary/hormones; friend said migraine so you tried triptans.

If clinician shows empathy/nonjudgment, you disclose the resisted items. Emphasize cost worries and fear meds will sedate you and impair newborn care/return to work.

medications: Sumatriptan 50 mg PO as needed for migraine (max 200 mg/day)
Combined oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel) 1 tablet PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Migraine with thunderclap-like headache can mimic RCVS presentation
Recent exposure to vasoactive agents (sumatriptan, SSRI) that can also trigger/worsen RCVS
Cannabis use (positive THC) associated with RCVS and complicates attribution of cause
Elevated blood pressure during severe pain may mimic hypertensive emergency-related headache
Early imaging can be normal; vasoconstriction may evolve over days, complicating initial diagnosis

differentials: pituitary deficiency due to Rathke's pouch cysts

required_evidence: thunderclap headache
recurrent severe headaches over days to weeks
normal or near-normal basic labs
no persistent focal neurologic deficit between attacks
trigger exposure (vasoactive drugs or postpartum) OR angiographic vasoconstriction that resolves within 3 months

medications: acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain
non_pharmacological: stop vasoactive triggers (triptans, ergotamines, decongestants, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs)
avoid glucocorticoids
blood pressure control (avoid hypotension)
urgent neuroimaging/CTA or MRA and neurology consultation
monitor for complications (ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, PRES)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: within 1 week (neurology); repeat vascular imaging in 6-12 weeks to document resolution

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms “reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome” and “thunderclap headache,” and frames RCVS as typically reversible but potentially complicated by stroke/SAH.

Communicates key red flags and urgency: recurrent thunderclap headaches, focal neurologic deficits, seizure, altered mental status, persistent/worsening severe headache, or new symptoms warrant emergent evaluation.

Explains treatment using the exact terms “calcium channel blocker” and “avoid triggers,” including common triggers (vasoactive substances such as triptans/ergots, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs in some cases, decongestants, energy stimulants; postpartum state; intense exertion/sexual activity; Valsalva).

Performs and documents the required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check (e.g., hypotension/bradycardia/heart block for non-dihydropyridine CCBs; pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations; hepatic impairment; concurrent antihypertensives).

Avoids contraindicated or potentially harmful therapies in suspected/confirmed RCVS, including routine use of glucocorticoids (may worsen outcomes) and vasoconstrictive migraine agents (triptans/ergots).

Provides practical medication counseling: expected benefits (headache reduction), common adverse effects (hypotension, dizziness, flushing, constipation/bradycardia depending on agent), and safety advice (orthostasis precautions, when to stop/seek care).
Encourages blood pressure monitoring and hydration as appropriate, and discusses individualized headache pain control that avoids vasoconstrictors.

Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty if applicable and notes the need to exclude mimics (aneurysmal SAH, cervical artery dissection, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, primary angiitis of CNS) while proceeding with safe initial management.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose RCVS and start a calcium channel blocker after mandatory safety checks, with trigger avoidance counseling and clear return precautions.
must_collect: thunderclap headache features (sudden onset, maximal intensity within 1 minute)
recurrent thunderclap episodes over days to weeks
neurologic symptoms (weakness, speech change, vision change, seizure, confusion)
exposure to vasoactive substances/medications (triptans/ergots, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants)
cardiovascular history relevant to CCB contraindications (bradycardia, heart block, hypotension, heart failure)
steps: Explain RCVS diagnosis and relationship to thunderclap headache; discuss reversibility and potential complications.
Counsel to avoid triggers/vasoactive substances and to stop vasoconstrictive headache meds (triptans/ergots).
Complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check prior to prescribing.
Prescribe a calcium channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine or verapamil) with dosing plan appropriate to local protocol and patient factors; provide side-effect and hypotension precautions.
Provide explicit emergency return precautions for red flags and worsening symptoms.

acceptable_variants.description: Uses an alternative calcium channel blocker selection (nimodipine vs verapamil vs nicardipine) tailored to patient comorbidities, still completing all required safety checks and counseling.
